questions.question: What best describes the process of differential diagnosis in the field?
options: A. Guessing the single correct diagnosis immediately
B. Narrowing a wide field of possible causes down to the most probable explanation(s)
C. Only ruling out cardiac causes
D. Waiting for hospital lab results before forming any impression
questions.rationale: Differential diagnosis is a process — moving from possibilities to probabilities using history, exam, and vitals — not a single instant guess.

questions.question: Which stage of assessment identifies and treats immediate life threats?
options: A. Scene size-up
B. Primary assessment
C. Secondary assessment
D. Reassessment
questions.rationale: The primary assessment establishes a general impression, level of consciousness, and identifies/treats airway, breathing, and circulation threats, also setting patient priority.

questions.question: In the medical patient, approximately what percentage of diagnostic information comes from the history?
questions.rationale: For medical patients, roughly 80% of the diagnostic information comes from the history, with about 20% from the physical exam — the reverse emphasis often seen in trauma.

questions.question: What should you do first when encountering a patient with an airway obstruction, before taking their full history?
options: A. Ask about past medical history
B. Treat the airway threat immediately
C. Obtain a complete medication list
D. Perform a detailed head-to-toe exam
questions.rationale: Life threats are treated first, regardless of how much history remains to be taken — airway, breathing, and circulation problems are addressed immediately in the primary assessment.

questions.question: You arrive at an apartment and find three family members all reporting headache, nausea, and dizziness that began around the same time. What should you suspect?
options: A. Three unrelated coincidental illnesses
B. A possible shared environmental/toxic exposure (e.g., carbon monoxide)
C. Anxiety spreading socially
D. Nothing unusual — treat each in isolation
questions.rationale: When multiple people share the same signs and symptoms, a common cause such as CO, a gas leak, or a shared food source should be suspected and investigated before entering blindly.

questions.question: What is included in the secondary assessment?
options: A. Scene safety only
B. History, physical examination, and vital signs
C. Airway management exclusively
D. Transport decision only
questions.rationale: The secondary assessment gathers history, performs a physical exam, and obtains vital signs — building the data needed for the differential diagnosis.

questions.question: Why is reassessment performed more frequently in unstable patients?
options: A. To satisfy documentation requirements
B. To track trends and detect deterioration or response to treatment promptly
C. It is not actually more frequent
D. Because unstable patients complain more
questions.rationale: Frequent reassessment in unstable patients catches trends — improving or worsening — allowing timely intervention changes.

questions.question: A family calls EMS saying their elderly father 'just isn't himself' but cannot name a specific complaint. What is the most useful question?
options: A. 'Do you have chest pain?' only
B. 'What is different today compared to his usual baseline?'
C. 'What is his diagnosis?'
D. No further questions are needed
questions.rationale: Asking about functional change from baseline (ADLs) often surfaces the real problem, especially when the patient or family cannot self-diagnose.

questions.question: What does 'trust YOUR exam' mean in patient assessment?
options: A. Ignore the patient's reported history entirely
B. Give weight to your own primary assessment and findings rather than discounting them in favor of a mismatched complaint
C. Only trust findings confirmed by a second provider
D. Physical exam findings are never reliable
questions.rationale: Your direct findings from the primary assessment are a critical, reliable data source — a mismatched or vague chief complaint shouldn't override what you actually observe.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the four stages of patient assessment?
options: A. Primary, scene size-up, secondary, reassessment
B. Scene size-up, primary assessment, secondary assessment, reassessment
C. Secondary, primary, reassessment, scene size-up
D. Reassessment, scene size-up, primary, secondary
questions.rationale: The stages proceed scene size-up → primary assessment → secondary assessment → reassessment, each building on the last.

questions.question: Why does the key to active listening involve getting on the patient's level?
options: A. It has no real effect on communication
B. It builds rapport and comfort, encouraging fuller, more accurate answers to follow-up questions
C. It is only a courtesy with no clinical value
D. It replaces the need for physical exam
questions.rationale: Physical positioning and engaged follow-up questions improve the quality and completeness of the history — the highest-yield tool in medical assessment.

questions.question: Which best explains why ADLs (Activities of Daily Living) matter in medical assessment?
options: A. They are irrelevant to EMS calls
B. A change in ADLs (walking, eating, dressing, thinking) is often the actual reason a call was made, even without a named diagnosis
C. ADLs only matter for pediatric patients
D. ADLs replace the need for vital signs
questions.rationale: Patients and families frequently call because functional status has changed, not because a diagnosis was made — asking about ADL changes captures this directly.

questions.question: During assessment of a conscious medical patient, what is the recommended order?
options: A. Physical exam first, then questions
B. Ask questions first, then perform a directed physical exam
C. Vital signs only, skip both history and exam
D. Simultaneously perform exam and questions with no particular order
questions.rationale: Given history's outsized diagnostic value in medical patients, asking questions first lets the subsequent physical exam be targeted and efficient.

questions.question: A crew skips checking for life threats and goes straight into a 10-minute history. What is the risk?
options: A. None — history is more important than life threats
B. A time-critical airway, breathing, or circulation problem may go untreated while history is gathered
C. This is the recommended approach for all patients
D. It only matters for trauma patients
questions.rationale: Regardless of history's diagnostic value, life threats must be identified and treated first — delaying this for history-taking risks patient deterioration.

questions.question: Which scenario best demonstrates appropriate use of the differential-diagnosis approach?
options: A. Assuming a single cause immediately and ignoring new exam findings
B. Building a list of possible causes, then using history/exam/vitals to raise or lower the likelihood of each
C. Never forming any clinical impression until arrival at the hospital
D. Diagnosing definitively in the field every time
questions.rationale: The differential-diagnosis approach is iterative: a list of possibilities is narrowed by evidence, not fixed on a single early guess or deferred entirely.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the scene size-up on a medical call?
options: A. To begin taking vital signs early
B. To assess safety, apply standard precautions, and identify clues to the nature of the illness before patient contact
C. To determine transport destination
D. To complete documentation
questions.rationale: Scene size-up happens before hands-on patient care and focuses on safety, precautions, and situational clues — protecting the responder and informing the coming assessment.

questions.question: What best describes the relationship between the four assessment stages?
options: A. They are independent and can be performed in any order
B. Each stage builds on the information and actions of the one before it
C. Only the primary assessment matters; the rest are optional
D. Reassessment replaces the need for a primary assessment
questions.rationale: Scene size-up informs safe approach, the primary assessment sets priority and treats life threats, the secondary assessment builds the differential, and reassessment tracks the response — each stage depends on the ones before it.

questions.question: A patient's chief complaint doesn't match your physical findings. What should guide your next action?
options: A. Always defer entirely to the stated chief complaint
B. Trust your own exam findings and investigate the discrepancy further
C. Ignore your findings since the patient knows their body best
D. Document the mismatch and take no further action
questions.rationale: Trusting your own primary assessment and findings — while still listening actively — is a core principle when the story and the exam don't align.
